scope.area: manager initial tenancy scan window that was observed near 60 seconds during runtime startup
coverage: measurement of startup scan stages before the fix
evaluation of competing hypotheses for the delay source
description of the RPC answer-routing fix applied
measurement of the same stages after the fix

summary.highLevelVerdict: The previously observed startup window of about 60 seconds was not caused by filesystem tenant scanning, multiple simultaneous scans, or pre-scan request pressure. The delay came from post-scan reconciliation RPC asks from manager to main whose answers were processed locally by main but were not routed back to manager, causing two back-to-back 30000ms timeouts. After adding local answer-routing fallback, the same startup section dropped from about 60042ms to about 1011ms.

observedProblem.symptom: Manager startup appeared to spend about 60 seconds inside the initial tenancy scan window.
impact: manager readiness was delayed long enough to destabilize startup perception
the earlier lifecycle run associated the window with route-resolution and smoke failures
the long delay obscured the real difference between scan time and reconciliation time
initialSignals: two measured startup windows landed near 60.05s each
the duration strongly resembled two chained 30000ms waits rather than one expensive scan

hypotheses.name: filesystem tenant scan itself is slow
hypotheses.reason: Direct instrumentation showed the tenant scan adapter completing in about 11ms.

hypotheses.name: multiple simultaneous scans are overlapping
hypotheses.reason: The measured delay concentrated in a single reconciliation phase after the scan, not in repeated adapter-scan execution.

hypotheses.name: pre-scan requests are consuming the startup window
hypotheses.reason: The same delay reproduced in manager-local reconciliation timing even without attributing cost to external request handling.

hypotheses.name: manager-to-main reconciliation RPC asks are timing out
hypotheses.reason: Instrumentation isolated about 60005ms inside process reconciliation, matching two 30000ms RPC ask timeouts. The main process handled the asks but answers did not route back to manager.

rootCause.shortVersion: RPC answers for manager-originated local-main reconciliation requests were not being routed back when direct answer delivery was unavailable.
details: During post-scan reconciliation, manager issued RPC asks such as process ensure/list operations to main.
Main processed those asks locally, but the answer path depended on direct delivery that was not available in this path.
Without an answer routed back to manager, each ask expired at the configured timeout of 30000ms.
Two such waits explained the observed startup window near 60 seconds.

changes: Added answer-routing fallback support in `app/_core/gateways/shared/rpc/rpc-endpoint.js` so locally processed asks can still return their answers through a router path.
Connected that fallback in `app/_core/gateways/shared/rpc/rpc-router.js` and guarded null or invalid route targets in the same flow.
Kept stage-level instrumentation in the tenancy router during diagnosis to separate scan cost from reconciliation cost.
Added a regression test in `app/test/future-system-improvements.test.js` covering local-main answer routing through the fallback path.

positive: The initial tenancy scan window dropped from about 60s to about 1s.
The bottleneck was narrowed to a specific RPC answer path and corrected rather than masked with timeout changes.
The final timing profile now shows tenant scanning as cheap and process reconciliation as bounded.
The regression test suite reached 70 passing tests after the fix.
residualNotes: This report isolates the 60-second startup bottleneck only; it does not by itself declare the entire TG2 smoke matrix complete.
Other runtime lifecycle issues should still be evaluated in their own follow-up validation passes.

tests.details: 70 tests passing after the RPC fallback fix and execution-meta follow-up work.

runtimeEvidence: Instrumented startup logs showed adapter scan around 11ms, cache invalidation around 5ms, reconciliation around 60005ms before the fix, and around 976ms after the fix.
The timing shift aligned with the removal of two chained 30000ms waits.

conclusion.operatorView: The startup delay was a real bottleneck but it was not in tenant scanning. It was an RPC answer-return path defect during manager-to-main reconciliation.
conclusion.engineeringView: The fix improved the measured scan window by about 59 seconds and converted a timeout-driven startup phase into a bounded sub-second-to-one-second reconciliation phase.
